explanation.text: Minna listens to Willie’s rich playing and thinks, “Willie on the street corner has a vibrato. Where is mine?” This comparison shows that watching him makes her realize she wants a vibrato of her own.

Minna knows Willie only as a street-corner player, but Porch reveals that Willie is a fine musician in a symphony chamber group. This new detail shows that Willie is more accomplished than he first appears.
Porch compares developing a vibrato to suddenly understanding a book for the first time. Therefore, “a light going on over your head” means gaining a new way of understanding something.
Porch explains that vibrato is not something that simply appears; Minna can work at it until understanding develops. This supports the theme because time and practice can help a person reach a goal.

Porch points out that Willie was already a fine musician before gaining vibrato, then says he and Minna can play wonderfully without it. Together, these statements show that vibrato is not required for Minna to play well.

Although Minna grumbles and worries about her vibrato, the story explicitly says she plays the cello because she wants to. Her smiling and playing wonderfully with Porch further show that she enjoys it.
The opening contrasts the modern world, where chocolate is widely eaten, with a time when only a few people drank it and no one ate it. This shows readers how rare chocolate once was.
The monkeys break open cacao pods and suck out the pulp, and a farmer later copies exactly what they do. This sequence explains that watching the monkeys is why farmers first tasted cacao pods.
Rotting food would normally be expected to smell unpleasant, yet these rotting cacao beans produce a delicious smell. Placing the phrases together therefore emphasizes an unexpected result.

After the steam engine was invented, factories could produce many goods more easily, including chocolate. Because production no longer depended on slow hand processing, chocolate became available beyond rich consumers.
The final paragraph says the Mayans knew they had found “something exciting” when they smelled the cacao beans. This direct statement best reveals their enthusiasm about the discovery.

The article moves from the first Central American farmers to the Mayans, Aztecs, Spanish explorers, European factories, and chocolate today. This sequence shows that events are presented in the order they happened.

Paragraph 4 details Sarah’s fear of the shark, but paragraph 5 says she is “scared but determined” and begins preparing to enter the water. This shows bravery because she chooses to act despite being afraid.
Sarah is excited and frightened, with a queasy stomach, shaking body, and weak limbs. Her answer comes out as a croak because fear makes it difficult for her to speak normally.
The story says Sarah’s experience swimming in the bay “paid off” when she slipped into the water and hardly made a ripple. This shows she knows how to enter quietly without disturbing the shark.

The tiger shark rests on the bottom only ten feet from Sarah and her grandfather without attacking them. This calm behavior directly contradicts the idea that sharks are killers that eat anything nearby.

Sarah imagines the shark’s senses and perspective, which helps her replace frightening legends with an understanding of its actual behavior. As a result, the next paragraphs show that she is no longer scared and follows fearlessly.

Sarah’s grandfather has safely worked with sharks for years, but he still recognizes the risks of any wild animal and gives careful instructions. This shows that he respects sharks rather than treating them as harmless.
The central action follows Sarah as she feels nervous, prepares carefully, and finally enters the water beside the shark. Therefore, her fear and readiness are essential to a summary of the story.
